title: What is an 'Intergovernmental Agreement' (IGA) for FATCA?

interlinking_suggestions.title: The Ultimate Digital Nomad Tax Survival Guide for 2025
A Guide to the 'Common Reporting Standard' (CRS) for Nomads
A guide to the Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA) for individuals

author.name: Ben Carter
author.bio: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Assume every foreign financial account is visible to the IRS, as Intergovernmental Agreements (IGAs) legally compel your bank to report your data.
Create a detailed inventory of all your foreign financial accounts and identify each country's IGA model to map the precise path your data travels to the IRS.
Proactively contact each foreign bank to confirm they have your correct U.S. TIN (SSN) on file, preventing the most common automated red flags caused by clerical errors.
Ensure your annual FBAR filing is a perfect mirror of the account data your foreign bank will report, as automated systems are designed to detect discrepancies.
Immediately provide your W-9 when requested by a foreign bank to avoid being labeled a 'recalcitrant account holder,' which risks account closure and loss of operational freedom.
summary: The FATCA Intergovernmental Agreement (IGA) has created a mandatory global system where foreign banks must report U.S. clients' financial data to the IRS, posing a significant compliance risk. To navigate this, you must proactively map your accounts to understand how your data travels and use a pre-compliance checklist to ensure the information banks report perfectly mirrors what you declare. This strategic approach transforms compliance from a source of anxiety into a manageable process, giving you control over your financial data and confidence in your global standing.
ai_overview_answer:
A FATCA Intergovernmental Agreement (IGA) is a formal treaty between the United States and another country that creates a legal framework for foreign banks to report information on their U.S. clients to the IRS. These agreements were created to resolve conflicts between U.S. FATCA reporting requirements and local data privacy laws. There are two main types: under a Model 1 IGA, the bank reports to its local tax authority which then sends the data to the IRS, while under a Model 2 IGA, the bank reports directly to the IRS. Compliance is enforced by a punitive 30% withholding tax on U.S. source payments to non-compliant institutions, making cooperation mandatory.

Foreign banks are required to report a client's name, U.S. Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN), account number, and year-end balance. Refusing to provide this information will cause the bank to label you a "recalcitrant account holder" and will likely lead to account closure. Therefore, individuals must ensure the information their bank has on file is accurate. This data must also perfectly mirror the information declared on U.S. filings like the Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts (FBAR) to prevent automated systems from flagging discrepancies.

author.name: Ben Carter
author.description: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

step.name: Map Your Compliance Footprint
step.text: Since this reporting is an absolute certainty, your first strategic move is to transform the abstract threat of global compliance into a tangible map you can control. You cannot manage a system you cannot see. This isn't about hiding; it's about clarity. By methodically identifying every channel through which your financial data flows, you replace vague anxiety with a clear operational picture. This is the foundational step to ensuring the information being reported about you is perfectly aligned with the information you report yourself.

itemListElement.text: Action: Create Your IGA Inventory. Open a secure spreadsheet or document. List every non-U.S. financial institution where you hold an account. This includes traditional banks and brokerages, but also the fintech platforms central to a global professional's life, like Wise, Revolut, or Deel. Go to the U.S. Treasury's official list of IGA jurisdictions to find the status of the country where each institution is located. Next to each entry, note its status: Model 1, Model 2, or no agreement.

Understand Your Data's Path. This inventory now gives you a precise roadmap. For your accounts in Model 1 IGA countries (like the United Kingdom, Canada, or Spain), you know the data goes from the bank to their local tax agency, which then forwards it to the IRS. For those in Model 2 countries (like Switzerland or Japan), you see a direct line from the financial institution straight to the IRS. This isn't just trivia; it tells you who is handling your personal data at each step of the tax information exchange.

Identify Your Highest-Risk Nodes. An account in a country with a long-standing FATCA IGA, like Germany or the UK, is part of a well-oiled reporting machine. The processes are mature and efficient. An account in a jurisdiction that more recently signed an agreement may have newer, less-tested procedures. The legal requirement to report is identical, but knowing the maturity of the system helps you prioritize where to proactively verify your information is correct, preventing errors before they become problems. This methodical mapping is critical because the old world of financial privacy is gone. As Pascal Saint-Amans, the former Head of Tax Policy at the OECD, bluntly stated, "bank secrecy is over." The system is designed to exchange information by default. Your only strategy is to own the data that is being shared.

step.name: The Pre-Compliance Checklist
step.text: Owning your data means ensuring its absolute integrity before it gets shared. The global tax information exchange system is an automated network designed specifically to spot mismatches between what your foreign bank reports about you and what you report about yourself. Discrepancies are the digital tripwires that trigger reviews. Your primary objective is to move from a reactive posture to one of proactive verification. This checklist is your personal risk-mitigation framework to guarantee the story your data tells is consistent, accurate, and entirely under your control.

itemListElement.text: Conduct a TIN/SSN Audit. Your U.S. Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN), usually your Social Security Number, is the primary key linking all reported data back to you. A simple clerical error can cause automated systems to flag your account. Proactively contact each of your foreign financial institutions. Frame it as a routine data integrity check: "I am conducting a review of my personal information on file to ensure its accuracy for upcoming reporting periods. Could you please confirm the U.S. TIN and full legal name you have associated with my accounts?" This positions you as a responsible, organized client and prevents the most common red flags.

Synchronize Your FBAR. The IGA framework compels your bank to report your account numbers and year-end balances. Separately, you have a legal duty to declare these same accounts to the U.S. Treasury if their aggregate value exceeds $10,000 at any point during the year by filing FinCEN Form 114, the "Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts" (FBAR). The IRS systems will compare the bank's incoming data with your FBAR filing. They must be a perfect mirror. Ensure every account is listed and every number is correct. Treat your FBAR not as a tax form, but as the master ledger that validates the data being shared about you.

Tell a Consistent Financial Story. The IRS is gaining unprecedented visibility into your global financial life. The critical question you must answer is: does the financial activity visible to them align with the income story you tell on your U.S. tax return? For example, if you claim the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion, the income you declare should be plausibly supported by the account balances and movements the IRS can now see. A large influx of cash into a foreign account that doesn't correspond with your reported earnings creates a narrative gap—and narrative gaps invite scrutiny.

Master "Recalcitrant" Status. When a foreign bank asks for your W-9, they are fulfilling their legal duty under their country's FATCA IGA. Refusing to provide this information gets you labeled a "recalcitrant account holder." This is not a passive designation; it is an active strategic failure that severely limits your operational freedom. Banks are not incentivized to protect you; they are required to report you. At best, they will report your account as non-compliant. At worst, they will close your account to rid themselves of the compliance headache. Providing your W-9 promptly is the single most important action you can take to maintain your good standing and control of your banking relationships.

mainEntity.name: Why is my foreign bank asking for my US Social Security Number?

acceptedAnswer.text: This request is not a sign of suspicion—it is a signal of a system working exactly as designed. Your foreign bank is complying with its legal obligations under the FATCA IGA between its home country and the United States. The agreement legally requires the bank to identify U.S. persons and report their financial information. Providing your U.S. TIN (your SSN) is simply the mechanism that allows them to fulfill their side of the treaty.

mainEntity.name: Does my bank in [Country] report my account to the IRS?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, almost certainly. With the United States having IGAs in effect with over 110 countries, the odds are overwhelmingly high that your bank is legally obligated to report your account information. Under a Model 1 IGA, your bank sends the data to its local tax authority, which then forwards it to the IRS. Under a Model 2 IGA, the bank reports directly. Either way, the information is transmitted. Assume every account you hold as a U.S. person in a partner country is visible.

mainEntity.name: What happens if I don't give my foreign bank my US tax information?

acceptedAnswer.text: Refusing to provide your information is a strategic error. The bank will classify you as a 'recalcitrant account holder.' A bank's primary incentive is to eliminate its own compliance risk, not to protect a non-cooperative client. This will likely lead to them closing your account to rid themselves of the liability. Maintaining control of your global banking infrastructure is critical, and prompt compliance is the key to preserving that operational freedom.

mainEntity.name: What information does my foreign bank send to the IRS under FATCA?

acceptedAnswer.text: The data reported is specific and standardized under the FATCA IGA framework to create a clear digital picture of your foreign holdings. It typically includes your full name and address, your U.S. Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN), the account number, and the year-end account balance or value. This information allows the IRS's automated systems to cross-reference the data received from the bank with the information you report on forms like the FBAR and Form 8938.

mainEntity.name: Is FATCA the same as the Common Reporting Standard (CRS)?

acceptedAnswer.text: They are related but distinct. Think of FATCA as a one-way street: financial data flows from around the world to the U.S. IRS. The Common Reporting Standard (CRS), on the other hand, is a multi-directional superhighway for tax information exchange. Over 100 countries have adopted CRS, agreeing to automatically exchange financial account information with each other. While the U.S. has not adopted CRS, if you are a resident of a CRS-participating country, your financial data is being shared even more broadly. This wider net makes a proactive approach to global compliance an absolute necessity.

body.content: FATCA's IGA Explained: A Playbook for Turning Global Compliance from a Threat into a Strategy

The new era of global financial transparency isn't built on trust; it's built on a global legal architecture known as the **FATCA Intergovernmental Agreement** (IGA). Understanding this framework is not an academic exercise—it is the essential first step to mastering your financial destiny. Think of this as your mission-critical intelligence report on the plumbing that connects your foreign bank accounts directly to the IRS.

This system was born from a fundamental conflict. The U.S. Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (FATCA) required foreign financial institutions (FFIs) to report on their U.S. clients, but doing so often violated their own countries' local privacy laws. This put banks in an impossible position: break local law or face severe penalties from the U.S.

The IGA is the diplomatic solution to this dilemma. It is a formal treaty between the United States and another country that creates a legal bridge, allowing your bank in Lisbon or your brokerage in Singapore to comply with FATCA without breaking their home country's laws. These agreements are the bedrock of modern **tax information exchange** and are what make your financial data securely portable across borders.
For you, the most crucial detail is the distinction between the two types of agreements, as it determines the precise path your data travels.

rows: Two-step: Bank -> Local Tax Authority -> IRS
Your financial institution reports to its own government's tax agency.
Your financial institution reports directly to the U.S. IRS.
Often reciprocal (Model 1A), meaning the U.S. also provides information back to the partner country.
Never reciprocal; it is a one-way flow of information to the U.S.

body.content: This entire system of **global compliance** is enforced by an incredibly powerful deterrent. Any financial institution that fails to comply faces a punitive **30% withholding tax** on certain payments it receives from U.S. sources. This penalty is so severe that it effectively forces cooperation. Your bank has zero financial incentive to shield you; its motivation is entirely aligned with full, accurate reporting of every U.S. account holder to protect its own financial health. They are not choosing to report you; they are compelled to.

Since this reporting is an absolute certainty, your first strategic move is to transform the abstract threat of **global compliance** into a tangible map you can control. You cannot manage a system you cannot see. This isn't about hiding; it's about clarity. By methodically identifying every channel through which your financial data flows, you replace vague anxiety with a clear operational picture. This is the foundational step to ensuring the information being reported about you is perfectly aligned with the information you report yourself.

items: **Action: Create Your IGA Inventory.** Open a secure spreadsheet or document. List every non-U.S. financial institution where you hold an account. This includes traditional banks and brokerages, but also the fintech platforms central to a global professional's life, like Wise, Revolut, or Deel. Go to the **U.S. Treasury's official list of IGA jurisdictions** to find the status of the country where each institution is located. Next to each entry, note its status: **Model 1**, **Model 2**, or no agreement.

**Understand Your Data's Path.** This inventory now gives you a precise roadmap. For your accounts in **Model 1** IGA countries (like the United Kingdom, Canada, or Spain), you know the data goes from the bank to their local tax agency, which then forwards it to the IRS. For those in **Model 2** countries (like Switzerland or Japan), you see a direct line from the financial institution straight to the IRS. This isn't just trivia; it tells you who is handling your personal data at each step of the **tax information exchange**.

**Identify Your Highest-Risk Nodes.** An account in a country with a long-standing **FATCA IGA**, like Germany or the UK, is part of a well-oiled reporting machine. The processes are mature and efficient. An account in a jurisdiction that more recently signed an agreement may have newer, less-tested procedures. The legal requirement to report is identical, but knowing the maturity of the system helps you prioritize where to proactively verify your information is correct, preventing errors before they become problems. This methodical mapping is critical because the old world of financial privacy is gone. As Pascal Saint-Amans, the former Head of Tax Policy at the OECD, bluntly stated, "bank secrecy is over." The system is designed to exchange information by default. Your only strategy is to own the data that is being shared.

The global **tax information exchange** system is an automated network designed specifically to spot mismatches between what your foreign bank reports about you and what you report about yourself. Discrepancies are the digital tripwires that trigger reviews. Your primary objective is to move from a reactive posture to one of proactive verification. This checklist is your personal risk-mitigation framework to guarantee the story your data tells is consistent, accurate, and entirely under your control.

A simple clerical error can cause automated systems to flag your account. Proactively contact each of your foreign financial institutions. Frame it as a routine data integrity check: "I am conducting a review of my personal information on file to ensure its accuracy for upcoming reporting periods. Could you please confirm the U.S. TIN and full legal name you have associated with my accounts?" This positions you as a responsible, organized client and prevents the most common red flags.

**Synchronize Your FBAR.** The IGA framework compels your bank to report your account numbers and year-end balances. Separately, you have a legal duty to declare these same accounts to the U.S. Treasury if their aggregate value exceeds $10,000 at any point during the year by filing FinCEN Form 114, the "Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts" (FBAR). The IRS systems will compare the bank's incoming data with your FBAR filing. They must be a perfect mirror. Ensure every account is listed and every number is correct. Treat your FBAR not as a tax form, but as the master ledger that validates the data being shared about you.

**Tell a Consistent Financial Story.** The IRS is gaining unprecedented visibility into your global financial life. Refusing to provide this information gets you labeled a "recalcitrant account holder." This is not a passive designation; it is an active strategic failure that severely limits your operational freedom. Banks are not incentivized to protect you; they are required to report you. At best, they will report your account as non-compliant. At worst, they will close your account to rid themselves of the compliance headache. Providing your W-9 promptly is the single most important action you can take to maintain your good standing and control of your banking relationships.

body.content: Executing your pre-compliance checklist often sparks deeper questions. This is a good sign—it shows you’re moving from abstract anxiety to proactive management. Here are the most common questions that surface for global professionals navigating this new landscape.

items:
**Why is my foreign bank asking for my US Social Security Number?**
This request is not a sign of suspicion—it is a signal of a system working exactly as designed. Your foreign bank is complying with its legal obligations under the **FATCA IGA** between its home country and the United States. The agreement legally requires the bank to identify U.S. persons and report their financial information. Providing your U.S. TIN (your SSN) is simply the mechanism that allows them to fulfill their side of the treaty.

**Does my bank in [Country] report my account to the IRS?**
Yes, almost certainly. With the United States having IGAs in effect with over 110 countries, the odds are overwhelmingly high that your bank is legally obligated to report your account information. Under a **Model 1** IGA, your bank sends the data to its local tax authority, which then forwards it to the IRS. Under a **Model 2** IGA, the bank reports directly. Either way, the information is transmitted. Assume every account you hold as a U.S. person in a partner country is visible.

**What happens if I don't give my foreign bank my US tax information?**
Refusing to provide your information is a strategic error. The bank will classify you as a "recalcitrant account holder." A bank's primary incentive is to eliminate its own compliance risk, not to protect a non-cooperative client. This will likely lead to them closing your account to rid themselves of the liability. Maintaining control of your global banking infrastructure is critical, and prompt compliance is the key to preserving that operational freedom.

**What information does my foreign bank send to the IRS under FATCA?**
The data reported is specific and standardized under the **FATCA IGA** framework to create a clear digital picture of your foreign holdings. It typically includes your full name and address, your U.S. Taxpayer Identification Number (TIN), the account number, and the year-end account balance or value. This information allows the IRS's automated systems to cross-reference the data received from the bank with the information you report on forms like the FBAR and Form 8938.

**Is FATCA the same as the Common Reporting Standard (CRS)?**
They are related but distinct. Think of FATCA as a one-way street: financial data flows from around the world *to* the U.S. IRS. The Common Reporting Standard (CRS), on the other hand, is a multi-directional superhighway for **tax information exchange**. Over 100 countries have adopted CRS, agreeing to automatically exchange financial account information *with each other*. While the U.S. has not adopted CRS, if you are a resident of a CRS-participating country, your financial data is being shared even more broadly. This wider net makes a proactive approach to **global compliance** an absolute necessity.

body.content: The Strategic Advantage: From Compliance Anxiety to Global Confidence

Understanding the **FATCA IGA** network accomplishes one critical goal: it eliminates the "unknown unknowns." The system is no longer an invisible, anxiety-inducing force. It is a predictable, rules-based network with clear inputs and outputs. That is a system you can manage. When you know precisely what information is flowing from your bank in Lisbon to its local tax authority and then on to the IRS, you drain the situation of its fear. It becomes a simple data-matching exercise.

This knowledge creates a fundamental shift in your posture. By mapping your footprint and proactively managing your data, you transition from being a passive subject of this system to its **active, strategic navigator**. The passive professional waits for the bank’s letter with a sense of dread. The strategic professional, knowing the **tax information exchange** is an automated reality, has already confirmed their U.S. TIN is correct on all accounts and ensured their FBAR filing is a perfect mirror of what the bank will send. This is the difference between being reactive and being in control.

This transformation turns **global compliance** from a source of chronic stress into a solved problem. The mental energy once spent worrying about hidden risks and potential penalties is now freed. This is the ultimate arbitrage opportunity for a global professional. You are recapturing your most valuable assets—your time, focus, and creative energy—to reinvest them where they generate real value: running your business, pursuing complex projects, and living the life of autonomy you chose. That is the ultimate form of control.
